summary:
The Pandya Dynasty (c. 300 BC–1345 AD, with multiple periods of dominance) was one of the oldest and most enduring Tamil kingdoms of South India, controlling the southernmost tip of the Indian subcontinent in what is today southern Tamil Nadu with its traditional capital at Madurai. The Pandyas are mentioned in ancient Greek, Roman, Chinese, and Arab sources as major participants in the Indian Ocean trade networks, exporting pearls, ivory, pepper, and fine muslin textiles to the Mediterranean and Southeast Asian markets from their ports on the Coromandel Coast and the Gulf of Mannar.

During the medieval period (c. 600–1300 AD), the Pandyas experienced alternating periods of ascendancy and subordination, competing with the [[event:2171|Chola Dynasty]] and the [[event:2173|Pallava Dynasty]] for dominance over South India. The Pandya literary tradition in Tamil is extraordinarily ancient — Sangam literature, composed between approximately 300 BC and 300 AD, is among the oldest surviving literature in any Dravidian language, and Madurai's legendary literary academy (Sangam) was the supposed venue for Tamil poetry's golden age. The Pandyas became a major power again after the decline of the Chola Empire in the 13th century, defeating the Cholas around 1279 AD and establishing themselves as the dominant force in South India until the Sultanate of Delhi's devastating invasions in the early 14th century — which sacked Madurai and the famous Meenakshi temple complex — effectively ended the dynasty's political power.
